did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used deep learning, specifically convolutional neural networks (CNNs), to evaluate robustness in skin cancer classification. The authors trained and evaluated four different CNN architectures on a benchmark of dermoscopic images (SAM, SAM-C, SAM-P) containing biopsy-verified melanoma and nevus images to assess classifier performance and susceptibility to out-of-distribution data.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper is explicitly labeled 'Original Research' on the first page. It presents original experimental work involving the creation of a new benchmark (SAM, SAM-C, SAM-P) using a proprietary database of biopsy-verified dermoscopic images and the evaluation of four CNN architectures to test robustness against out-of-distribution data.

title: A benchmark for neural network robustness in skin cancer classification
brief_summary: This study established a dermoscopic skin cancer benchmark to evaluate the robustness of neural network classifiers against out-of-distribution data. Using a proprietary database of biopsy-verified images, the authors created three datasets: SAM (319 unmodified melanoma and nevus images), SAM-C (corrupted images), and SAM-P (perturbed images). Four convolutional neural network architectures were trained and evaluated on this benchmark to assess their susceptibility to image corruptions and perturbations. The results demonstrated that all tested CNNs were susceptible to these modifications, highlighting common shortcomings in current deep learning models. The benchmark is publicly available without ground truth labels to maintain its out-of-distribution status and facilitate thorough future evaluations. Ultimately, this work aims to enable the development of more robust skin cancer classifiers by providing a standardized frame of reference for OOD testing.

lesion_types_studied.details: Biopsy-verified dermoscopic images of melanoma (n=194) from 37 unique lesions were included in the SAM dataset.
Biopsy-verified dermoscopic images of nevus (n=125) from 48 unique lesions were included in the SAM dataset.

all_task_types.details: The paper evaluates binary skin cancer classifiers on dermoscopic images. The model predicts discrete classes: melanoma (n=194) or nevus (n=125). Performance is measured using balanced accuracy and balanced error rate, which are standard metrics for classification tasks.

datasets_used.name: SAM (Skin Archive Munich)
datasets_used.description: A proprietary database of biopsy-verified dermoscopic images compiled at the Department of Dermatology and Allergy, University Hospital, LMU Munich, Germany. It contains unmodified images from 85 unique lesions (37 melanoma, 48 nevus) photographed with a NIKON D2X and scaled to 1000x667 pixels.

use.training: Not used for training; ground truth labels are not provided to maintain OOD status. Models are trained on external datasets (HAM10000, ISIC, BCN20000, PH2, DERM7PT, SKINL2).
use.testing: Used as the clean/unmodified test set to establish baseline performance (balanced accuracy) for the benchmark.
use.other: Source for generating SAM-C and SAM-P datasets.

datasets_used.name: SAM-C (SAM-corrupted)
datasets_used.description: A dataset derived from SAM containing images artificially modified with 22 types of corruptions (noise, blur, dermoscopy, digital) across five severity levels to mimic low-quality inputs (e.g., blurry, obstructed, distorted).

use.testing: Used to test classifier robustness against corruptions by computing balanced error rates across corruption types and severity levels.
use.other: Part of the OOD robustness benchmark.

datasets_used.name: SAM-P (SAM-perturbed)
datasets_used.description: A dataset derived from SAM containing images modified with perturbation sequences (31 frames per image) across four categories (noise, blur, dermoscopy, digital) to test prediction stability over small image changes.

use.testing: Used to test classifier stability against perturbations.
use.other: Part of the OOD robustness benchmark.

datasets_used.name: HAM10000
datasets_used.description: A large collection of multi-source dermatoscopic images of common pigmented skin lesions, used as a source for training the CNN architectures.
classes_used: Benign and malignant pigmented skin lesions

use.training: Used for training the four CNN architectures (AlexNet, VGG16+BN, ResNet50, DenseNet121).
use.testing: Not used for testing in this study.

datasets_used.name: ISIC
datasets_used.description: International Skin Imaging Collaboration dataset, used as a source for training the CNN architectures.

use.training: Used for training the four CNN architectures.
use.testing: Not used for testing in this study.

datasets_used.name: BCN20000
datasets_used.description: Dermoscopic lesions dataset, used as a source for training the CNN architectures.

use.training: Used for training the four CNN architectures.
use.testing: Not used for testing in this study.

datasets_used.name: PH2
datasets_used.description: Dermoscopic image dataset, used as a source for training the CNN architectures.

use.training: Used for training the four CNN architectures.
use.testing: Not used for testing in this study.

datasets_used.name: DERM7PT
datasets_used.description: Dermoscopic image dataset, used as a source for training the CNN architectures.

use.training: Used for training the four CNN architectures.
use.testing: Not used for testing in this study.

datasets_used.name: SKINL2
datasets_used.description: Dermoscopic image dataset, used as a source for training the CNN architectures.

use.training: Used for training the four CNN architectures.
use.testing: Not used for testing in this study.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance comparisons between models were based on descriptive metrics such as balanced accuracy, balanced error rate, and flip rate without statistical significance testing.

AlexNet.note: Worst performance among the four models.

VGG16+BN.note: Best performance among the four models.

ResNet50.note: Best performance among the four models.

DenseNet121.note: Performance similar to VGG16+BN and ResNet50.

SAM (Clean).notes: Specific balanced accuracy values are not reported in the text; they are referenced in Figure 3 and Supplementary Table 1. VGG16+BN and ResNet50 had the best performance, while AlexNet had the worst.

SAM-C (Corrupted).metric: Mean Balanced Corruption Error (mBCE) and Relative mBCE

AlexNet.note: Used as the baseline for relative mBCE calculation. Had the highest relative mBCE.

VGG16+BN.note: Lower mBCE than AlexNet but substantially higher relative mBCE.

ResNet50.note: Lower mBCE than AlexNet but substantially higher relative mBCE.

DenseNet121.note: Lowest mBCE and lowest relative mBCE, indicating best corruption robustness.

SAM-C (Corrupted).notes: Specific mBCE and relative mBCE values are not reported in the text; they are referenced in Figure 3 and Supplementary Table 1. DenseNet121 showed the best corruption robustness. VGG16+BN and ResNet50 performed worse than AlexNet in terms of relative mBCE, suggesting their improved mBCE was due to overall accuracy gains rather than robustness.

AlexNet.note: Lowest mFR (best stability) among the four models.

ResNet50.note: Higher mFR than AlexNet. Had a 6.7% probability of changing its prediction between adjacent frames.

SAM-P (Perturbed).notes: Specific mFR values are not reported in the text; they are referenced in Figure 3 and Supplementary Table 1. AlexNet showed better stability (lower mFR) than the other models. ResNet50 had a 6.7% probability of changing its prediction between adjacent frames.

architecture_used.main_model: Four convolutional neural network (CNN) architectures: AlexNet, VGG16+BN, ResNet50, and DenseNet121.

training_details.data_augmentation: Training was carried out without the data augmentations used to create SAM-C and SAM-P (i.e., no rotations, tilts, Gaussian noise, etc.).
training_details.repetitions: Training and evaluation were repeated five times for each architecture to obtain an averaged result.

preprocessing_techniques: Artificial corruptions applied to SAM-C dataset: Gaussian noise, Shot noise, Impulse noise, Speckle noise, Defocus blur, Motion blur, Zoom blur, Gaussian blur, Black corners, Characters overlay, Bubbles overlay, Brightness up/down, Contrast adjustment, Elastic transformation, Pixelation, JPEG compression, Saturation adjustment
Artificial perturbations applied to SAM-P dataset: Translate, Rotate, Tilt, Scale, Shear
Training performed without data augmentations (no rotations, tilts, Gaussian noise, etc.)

data_augmentation_techniques.details: The paper explicitly states that the four CNN architectures (AlexNet, VGG16+BN, ResNet50, DenseNet121) were trained 'without data augmentation' on the external training datasets (HAM10000, ISIC, BCN20000, PH2, DERM7PT, SKINL2). The corruptions and perturbations described (SAM-C and SAM-P) were applied to the test/benchmark datasets to evaluate robustness, not used as training data augmentation.

conclusions: The study established a dermoscopic skin cancer benchmark consisting of three datasets (SAM, SAM-C, and SAM-P) to enable out-of-distribution (OOD) testing of binary skin cancer classifiers.

All four tested convolutional neural network (CNN) architectures (AlexNet, VGG16+BN, ResNet50, and DenseNet121) demonstrated susceptibility to image corruptions and perturbations, confirming common shortcomings in current deep learning models.

While VGG16+BN and ResNet50 showed improved balanced accuracy on clean images compared to AlexNet, their relative balanced corruption error was substantially higher, indicating that accuracy improvements can mask underlying corruption robustness issues.
DenseNet121 exhibited the best corruption robustness, having the lowest mean balanced corruption error (mBCE) and relative mBCE among the tested models.
AlexNet demonstrated better stability against perturbations (lowest mean flip rate) compared to the other architectures, although it had the worst performance on clean images.
The benchmark is made publicly available without ground truth labels to maintain its OOD status and prevent data leakage, with test results to be submitted to the authors for assessment.
The benchmark provides a frame of reference to facilitate a more thorough evaluation process and enable the development of more robust skin cancer classifiers.
important_remarks: The benchmark dataset (SAM) is proprietary and biopsy-verified, containing 319 images (194 melanoma, 125 nevus) from 85 unique lesions, which enhances clinical relevance compared to public datasets.
Ground truth labels are intentionally withheld to maintain the out-of-distribution (OOD) status of the benchmark and prevent data leakage; test results must be submitted to authors for assessment.

Training was explicitly performed without data augmentations (e.g., no rotations, tilts, or Gaussian noise) to ensure that any robustness observed is not an artifact of training-time augmentation overlap with the corruption types in SAM-C.

All four tested CNN architectures (AlexNet, VGG16+BN, ResNet50, DenseNet121) demonstrated susceptibility to image corruptions and perturbations, highlighting a common shortcoming in current deep learning models.

Improved balanced accuracy on clean images (seen in VGG16+BN and ResNet50) can mask underlying corruption robustness issues, as evidenced by their substantially higher relative balanced corruption error compared to AlexNet.

DenseNet121 exhibited the best corruption robustness (lowest mean balanced corruption error), while AlexNet showed the best stability against perturbations (lowest mean flip rate), indicating a trade-off between accuracy and stability.
The study relies on artificial corruptions and perturbations rather than purely external clinical datasets to test robustness, which may not fully capture all real-world distribution shifts.
No formal statistical hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values) was reported; performance comparisons were based on descriptive metrics such as balanced accuracy, balanced error rate, and flip rate.

Several authors report significant conflicts of interest, including advisory roles, honoraria, and consulting fees from pharmaceutical companies (e.g., Novartis, Roche, BMS, Pierre Fabre) and ownership of telemedicine companies, which may introduce bias.
